title: dsHPC Package Integration Plan
text: dsHPC package designed used exclusively within DataSHIELD environment submit jobs Slurm. However, current codebase two parallel sets functions: Regular functions (dsHPC.init, dsHPC.submit, etc.) DataSHIELD-specific functions (dsHPC.ds.init, dsHPC.ds.submit, etc.) duplication creates confusion maintenance overhead. document outlines plan streamline codebase.

title: dsHPC Package Integration Plan
text: Unified Interface: package single set functions always operate DataSHIELD context. Direct Slurm Integration: job submissions target Slurm (local execution fallback unavailable). Simplified Code Structure: Remove redundant DS-specific wrappers integrate DataSHIELD-specific logic main functions.

title: dsHPC Package Integration Plan
text: Check running DataSHIELD environment (using is_datashield_env()) , issue warning package designed DataSHIELD Continue initialization note features may work outside DataSHIELD Check running DataSHIELD environment Incorporate DataSHIELD-specific logic currently wrapper functions Proceed submission Slurm (local execution available)

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: dsHPC package provides seamless interface DataSHIELD server-side operations High-Performance Computing (HPC) clusters. enables computationally intensive analyses offloaded HPC resources, caching mechanisms avoid redundant calculations. vignette introduces basic usage package end-users developers creating DataSHIELD packages.

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: typical workflow using dsHPC consists : Initializing system Submitting jobs Checking job status Retrieving results

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: using functions package, need initialize : sets database connection caching checks available job schedulers. Slurm cluster available, package fallback local execution.
code: library(dsHPC) dsHPC.init()

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: can submit function executed HPC cluster: complex operations, can specify additional parameters: Alternatively, can use dsHPC.submit_by_name submit job function name:
code: # Submit a simple job job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = mean,  # Pass the actual function   args = list(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # The job object contains information about the submission job # Submit a job with more options job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = kmeans,   args = list(x = my_data_matrix, centers = 3),   required_packages = c("stats"),   use_cache = TRUE,   slurm_opts = list(     partition = "normal",     memory = "8G",     time = "02:00:00",     cpus = 2   ) ) # Submit using function name job <- dsHPC.submit_by_name(   func_name = "kmeans",   args = list(x = my_data_matrix, centers = 3),   package = "stats",   required_packages = c("stats") )

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: can check status submitted job: status one : - SUBMITTED: Job submitted scheduler - PENDING: Job waiting executed - RUNNING: Job currently running - COMPLETED: Job completed successfully - FAILED: Job failed - CACHED: Result retrieved cache
code: # Get job status status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id) status

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: job completed, can retrieve results: can also wait job complete:
code: # Retrieve the result result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id) # Wait for completion and get result result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE, timeout = 300)

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: One key features dsHPC intelligent caching system, avoids redundant computations reusing results previous calls identical inputs.

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: dsHPC uses advanced hashing mechanism uniquely identify jobs based : Function definition: actual implementation function considered, just function name Arguments: exact arguments passed function Environment: Relevant context function runs
code: # Define a custom function my_add <- function(a, b) { a + b }  # First execution will compute the result job1 <- dsHPC.submit(   func = my_add,   args = list(a = 1, b = 2) ) # Status is COMPLETED, result is computed job1$status #> [1] "COMPLETED"  # Second execution with identical inputs uses the cache job2 <- dsHPC.submit(   func = my_add,   args = list(a = 1, b = 2) ) # Status is CACHED, result is retrieved from cache job2$status #> [1] "CACHED"  # Even functionally identical functions will use the cache # Define another function with the same behavior but different formatting my_add2 <- function(a, b) {    # Different formatting, but same functionality   a + b  }  job3 <- dsHPC.submit(   func = my_add2,   args = list(a = 1, b = 2) ) # Status is CACHED, result is retrieved from cache job3$status #> [1] "CACHED"

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: can manually clean cache remove old results:
code: # Remove results older than 14 days dsHPC.clean_cache(days_to_keep = 14)  # Clear all completed job results dsHPC.clean_cache(days_to_keep = 0)

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: can create wrapped version function automatically executed via HPC: particularly useful want transparently offload computation-intensive functions HPC resources.
code: # Create a wrapped version of the kmeans function kmeans_hpc <- dsHPC.wrap_function(   func = kmeans,   package = "stats",   required_packages = c("stats"),   slurm_opts = list(memory = "8G", cpus = 2) )  # Use the wrapped function as you would use the original function result <- kmeans_hpc(x = my_data, centers = 3)

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: ’re developing DataSHIELD server-side package want leverage HPC capabilities, can integrate dsHPC follows: Add dsHPC dependency package Initialize dsHPC needed Use dsHPC.submit() dsHPC.wrap_function() execute computation-intensive functions Example: approach allows package benefit HPC resources maintaining simple API end users.
code: #' My intensive analysis function #'  #' @param data Data frame to analyze #' @param ... Additional parameters #' @export my_intensive_analysis <- function(data, ...) {   # Initialize dsHPC if not already done   if (is.null(getOption("dsHPC.config"))) {     dsHPC::dsHPC.init()   }      # Submit the actual analysis function to HPC   job <- dsHPC::dsHPC.submit(     func_name = "actual_analysis_implementation",     args = list(data = data, ...),     package = "myPackage"   )      # Wait for and return the result   dsHPC::dsHPC.result(job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE) }

title: Introduction to dsHPC
text: dsHPC package provides powerful flexible interface offloading computational tasks HPC resources within DataSHIELD framework. handling job submission, monitoring, result caching, enables efficient use computing resources maintaining ease use. detailed information specific functions, please refer package documentation.

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: dsHPC package provides comprehensive job management capabilities help track interact computational jobs throughout lifecycle. vignette demonstrates monitor, query, manage R Python jobs across various states.

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: Jobs dsHPC can exist one following states: SUBMITTED: job submitted scheduler processing queue PENDING: job waiting scheduler queue RUNNING: job currently executing COMPLETED: job finished successfully FAILED: job encountered error execution CANCELLED: job manually cancelled CACHED: result retrieved cache (real job state, reported status function)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: managing jobs, need initialize dsHPC environment:
code: library(dsHPC) dsHPC.init()

title: Job Management with dsHPC
code: # Define a function that takes some time to run long_computation <- function(size) {   # Simulate a long computation   result <- matrix(rnorm(size^2), size, size)   # Compute eigenvectors (computationally intensive for large matrices)   eigen_result <- eigen(result)   return(eigen_result$values) }  # Submit the job r_job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = long_computation,   args = list(size = 1000),   slurm_opts = list(     memory = "4G",     cpus = 2   ) )  # The job is now submitted and has a unique ID print(r_job$job_id)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
code: # For Python jobs, we use dsHPC.submit_python py_job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "sklearn.cluster",   py_function = "KMeans",   args = list(     n_clusters = 5,     random_state = 42   ),   required_modules = c("numpy", "sklearn") )  # Python jobs also receive a unique ID print(py_job$job_id)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: basic task job management checking current status job: dsHPC.status() function returns list containing information job, including: job_id: unique identifier job job_hash: hash based function arguments status: current state job (“SUBMITTED”, “PENDING”, “RUNNING”, “COMPLETED”, “FAILED”, “CANCELLED”) result: job result, available requested (see )
code: # Check status of R job r_status <- dsHPC.status(r_job$job_id) print(r_status)  # Check status of Python job py_status <- dsHPC.status(py_job$job_id) print(py_status)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: job completed, can retrieve result:
code: # Basic result retrieval (non-blocking) if (r_status$status == "COMPLETED") {   result <- dsHPC.result(r_job$job_id)   print(head(result))  # Show the first few eigenvalues }

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: can also wait job complete retrieve result single call: wait_for_completion parameter causes function block job completes timeout reached. timeout reached, function returns NULL issues warning.
code: # Waiting for completion (blocking) result <- dsHPC.result(r_job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE)  # You can also specify a timeout in seconds result <- dsHPC.result(py_job$job_id,                         wait_for_completion = TRUE,                         timeout = 300)  # 5-minute timeout

what: Checking Completion Status and Result in One Call
title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: can retrieve job status result (available) single call:
code: # Check status and get result if available status_with_result <- dsHPC.status(r_job$job_id, return_result = TRUE)  if (status_with_result$status == "COMPLETED") {   print(head(status_with_result$result)) }

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: can list jobs filter status: dsHPC.list_jobs() function returns data frame containing information jobs match filter criteria.
code: # List all jobs all_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs() print(all_jobs)  # List only completed jobs completed_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs(status_filter = "COMPLETED") print(completed_jobs)  # List jobs that are either running or pending active_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs(status_filter = c("RUNNING", "PENDING")) print(active_jobs)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: want stop job ’s currently running pending: Note job cancellation works job cancellable state (SUBMITTED, PENDING, RUNNING) job scheduler available. simulation mode, jobs already completed failed, cancellation work.
code: # Cancel a running job if (r_status$status %in% c("RUNNING", "PENDING")) {   cancelled <- dsHPC.cancel(r_job$job_id)   if (cancelled) {     print("Job cancelled successfully")   } else {     print("Failed to cancel job")   } }

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: job fails, can still retrieve information failure:
code: # Check if a job failed if (py_status$status == "FAILED") {   # Get detailed job information   py_job_info <- get_job_info(getOption("dsHPC.config")$connection, py_job$job_id)      # Print the error message   print(paste("Job failed with error:", py_job_info$error_message)) }

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: ’s practical workflow managing multiple jobs:
code: # Submit multiple jobs jobs <- list() for (i in 1:5) {   jobs[[i]] <- dsHPC.submit(     func = function(n) sum(rnorm(n)^2),     args = list(n = i * 1000)   ) }  # Monitor all jobs until completion monitor_until_completion <- function(job_list, interval = 2, timeout = 300) {   start_time <- Sys.time()   while (TRUE) {     # Check all jobs     all_completed <- TRUE     for (i in seq_along(job_list)) {       status <- dsHPC.status(job_list[[i]]$job_id)       cat(sprintf("Job %d: %s\n", i, status$status))              if (!status$status %in% c("COMPLETED", "FAILED", "CANCELLED", "CACHED")) {         all_completed <- FALSE       }     }          # If all jobs are in a final state, exit the loop     if (all_completed) {       break     }          # Check timeout     elapsed <- difftime(Sys.time(), start_time, units = "secs")     if (elapsed > timeout) {       warning("Timeout reached while waiting for job completion")       break     }          # Wait before checking again     Sys.sleep(interval)   }      # Collect all results   results <- list()   for (i in seq_along(job_list)) {     status <- dsHPC.status(job_list[[i]]$job_id)     if (status$status == "COMPLETED" || status$status == "CACHED") {       results[[i]] <- dsHPC.result(job_list[[i]]$job_id)     } else {       results[[i]] <- NULL     }   }      return(results) }  # Monitor all jobs and collect results results <- monitor_until_completion(jobs) print(results)

what: Implementing Job Management in DataSHIELD Packages
title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: ’re developing DataSHIELD package uses dsHPC, consider implementing job management patterns:
code: # Example of a function for a DataSHIELD package myPackage.submitAnalysis <- function(data, params) {   # Check if dsHPC is available   if (!requireNamespace("dsHPC", quietly = TRUE) ||        is.null(getOption("dsHPC.config"))) {     stop("dsHPC is not available or initialized")   }      # Submit the job   job <- dsHPC.submit(     func = function(data, params) {       # Actual computation code       result <- analyze_data(data, params)       return(result)     },     args = list(data = data, params = params),     use_cache = TRUE   )      # Return job ID and info for status checking   return(list(     job_id = job$job_id,     status = job$status,     message = "Analysis job submitted successfully. Use myPackage.checkAnalysis() to check status."   )) }  # Function to check analysis status myPackage.checkAnalysis <- function(job_id) {   if (!requireNamespace("dsHPC", quietly = TRUE)) {     stop("dsHPC is not available")   }      status <- dsHPC.status(job_id)      if (status$status == "COMPLETED" || status$status == "CACHED") {     return(list(       status = status$status,       message = "Analysis completed. Use myPackage.getAnalysisResult() to retrieve results."     ))   } else if (status$status == "FAILED") {     job_info <- dsHPC:::get_job_info(getOption("dsHPC.config")$connection, job_id)     return(list(       status = "FAILED",       message = paste("Analysis failed:", job_info$error_message)     ))   } else {     return(list(       status = status$status,       message = paste("Analysis is", tolower(status$status))     ))   } }  # Function to get analysis result myPackage.getAnalysisResult <- function(job_id) {   if (!requireNamespace("dsHPC", quietly = TRUE)) {     stop("dsHPC is not available")   }      # Check status first   status <- dsHPC.status(job_id)      if (!(status$status %in% c("COMPLETED", "CACHED"))) {     stop(paste("Cannot retrieve result. Job status is", status$status))   }      # Get the result   result <- dsHPC.result(job_id)   return(result) }

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: job management functions work identically R Python jobs. allows manage mix R Python jobs using single consistent interface.
code: # Submit both R and Python jobs r_job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = function(x) mean(x),   args = list(x = rnorm(1000)) )  py_job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = rnorm(1000)) )  # Check status of both jobs r_status <- dsHPC.status(r_job$job_id) py_status <- dsHPC.status(py_job$job_id)  # Retrieve results r_result <- dsHPC.result(r_job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE) py_result <- dsHPC.result(py_job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE)  # List all jobs of any language all_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs()

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: Always check job status retrieving results Use timeouts waiting job completion Implement error handling job failures Regularly clean old jobs results Maintain job IDs long-running processes Store job IDs persistent location (e.g., database file) monitoring long-running jobs may span multiple R sessions.
code: status <- dsHPC.status(job_id) if (status$status == "COMPLETED") {   result <- dsHPC.result(job_id) } result <- dsHPC.result(job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE, timeout = 3600)  # 1 hour status <- dsHPC.status(job_id) if (status$status == "FAILED") {   # Handle the failure, perhaps by resubmitting or using a fallback method } # Remove results older than 14 days dsHPC.clean_cache(days_to_keep = 14)

title: Job Management with dsHPC
text: Effective job management essential making high-performance computing capabilities dsHPC. functions patterns demonstrated vignette allow submit, monitor, retrieve results R Python jobs, providing unified interface computational tasks across languages.

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: dsHPC package provides seamless integration Python reticulate package. allows run Python code HPC resources, automatic conversion R Python data types.

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: using Python integration features, ensure : working Python installation reticulate R package installed Python modules code requires
code: library(dsHPC) library(reticulate)

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: submitting jobs, need initialize dsHPC system:
code: dsHPC.init()

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: dsHPC.submit_python() function allows submit Python function executed job. ’s simple example using NumPy:
code: # Submit a job to calculate the mean of some numbers using NumPy job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Check the job status status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id) print(status)  # Get the result when the job is complete if (status$status == "COMPLETED") {   result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id)   print(result) }

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: can specify Python interpreter use:
code: # Using a specific Python executable job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)),   python_path = "/path/to/python" )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
code: # Using a virtual environment job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)),   virtualenv = "my_venv" )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
code: # Using a conda environment job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)),   condaenv = "my_conda_env" )

what: Passing Data Between R and Python
title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: dsHPC package automatically converts R objects Python back. examples:

previous_headings: Passing Data Between R and Python
title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: R vectors converted NumPy arrays:
code: # R vector to NumPy array and back r_vector <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = r_vector) )  # Result is an R scalar (3) result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id)

previous_headings: Passing Data Between R and Python
title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: R data frames converted pandas DataFrames:
code: # Create a sample data frame df <- data.frame(   x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),   y = c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Submit job to process the data frame job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "pandas",   py_function = "DataFrame",   args = list(data = df) )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: Just like R functions, dsHPC includes intelligent caching system Python functions. system identifies Python jobs based : Python module name Python function name function’s implementation (available) exact arguments passed function Python function called arguments, cached result returned immediately without re-executing function:
code: # First execution of a Python function job1 <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Status is COMPLETED, result is computed job1$status #> [1] "COMPLETED"  # Second execution with identical inputs uses the cache job2 <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Status is CACHED, result is retrieved from cache job2$status #> [1] "CACHED"

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: Python functions, dsHPC attempts capture function’s bytecode accurate comparison. means functionally identical Python functions (even ’re imported differently accessed different paths) can recognized caching purposes.

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: need run Python function without using cache (e.g., time-sensitive data non-deterministic functions), can disable caching:
code: # Disable caching for this job job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)),   use_cache = FALSE )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: can specify additional Python modules need imported:
code: # Submit a job that requires multiple modules job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "matplotlib.pyplot",   py_function = "plot",   args = list(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5), y = c(10, 20, 15, 30, 25)),   required_modules = c("numpy", "pandas") )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: can specify Slurm options Python jobs:
code: # Submit a job with specific Slurm requirements job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "linalg.eig",  # Computationally intensive function   args = list(a = matrix(rnorm(100*100), 100, 100)),   slurm_opts = list(     partition = "normal",     memory = "4g",     time = "00:30:00",     cpus = 4   ) )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC
text: can also call functions Python modules:
code: # Assuming you have a custom module named "my_module.py" # with a function called "process_data" job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "my_module",   py_function = "process_data",   args = list(     data = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),     options = list(normalize = TRUE)   ) )

title: Python Integration with dsHPC

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: dsHPC server-side DataSHIELD package enables execution High-Performance Computing (HPC) jobs within DataSHIELD framework. provides standardized interface submitting, monitoring, retrieving results jobs running HPC resources (Slurm clusters), built-caching avoid redundant computations. package designed used exclusively within DataSHIELD server-side environment.

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: DataSHIELD Integration: Fully integrated DataSHIELD privacy-preserving framework HPC Integration: Direct interface Slurm job scheduler submit manage computational jobs Efficient Caching: Store retrieve results based function parameter hashing avoid redundant computations Job Management: Submit, monitor, cancel, retrieve results computational jobs Flexible Configuration: Customizable job submission parameters (memory, CPUs, time limits, etc.) Local Fallback: Gracefully falls back local execution Slurm unavailable Python Integration: Seamlessly execute Python code modules, support image processing via Pillow

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: Package Website - Complete documentation function reference vignettes Usage Guide - Comprehensive documentation using dsHPC Developer Integration Guide - Guide DataSHIELD package developers want integrate dsHPC Docker Setup - Example Dockerfile set dsHPC requirements rock instance

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: can install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("isglobal-brge/dsHPC")

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: using package, need initialize : creates necessary database connections checks Slurm scheduler. package always attempt use Slurm job submission, falling back local execution Slurm unavailable.
code: library(dsHPC) dsHPC.init()

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: Submit function executed Slurm cluster:
code: # Simple example with function reference job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = mean,   args = list(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Using a function name job <- dsHPC.submit_by_name(   func_name = "kmeans",   args = list(x = my_data_matrix, centers = 3),   package = "stats" )  # With custom Slurm options job <- dsHPC.submit(   func = kmeans,   args = list(x = my_data_matrix, centers = 3),   slurm_opts = list(     partition = "normal",     memory = "8G",     time = "02:00:00",     cpus = 2   ) )

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: Submit Python code functions:
code: # Submit a Python function from a module job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   py_module = "numpy",   py_function = "mean",   args = list(a = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Submit arbitrary Python code job <- dsHPC.submit_python(   python_code = " import numpy as np result = np.mean([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]) " )

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: Check status submitted job:
code: status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id)

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: Get result completed job:
code: # Get the result (returns NULL if not completed) result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id)  # Check status and get result status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id, return_result = TRUE) if (status$status == "COMPLETED") {   result <- status$result   print(result) }

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: List jobs filter status: Cancel running job: Clean old cached results:
code: # List all jobs all_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs()  # List only completed jobs completed_jobs <- dsHPC.list_jobs(status_filter = "COMPLETED") dsHPC.cancel(job$job_id) dsHPC.clean_cache(days_to_keep = 14)

title: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing
text: DataSHIELD server-side packages can leverage dsHPC : Adding dsHPC dependency Using dsHPC.submit() execute computationally intensive functions Example integration another package: advanced integration patterns, see Developer Integration Guide.
code: # In your package's function my_intensive_analysis <- function(data, ...) {   # Check if dsHPC is available   if (requireNamespace("dsHPC", quietly = TRUE) && !is.null(getOption("dsHPC.config"))) {     # Define the computation function     compute_function <- function(data, ...) {       # Intensive computation       result <- # ...       return(result)     }          # Submit to HPC     job <- dsHPC::dsHPC.submit(       func = compute_function,       args = list(data = data, ...),       use_cache = TRUE     )          # Wait for and return the result     if (job$status == "COMPLETED" || job$status == "CACHED") {       return(job$result)     } else {       # Wait for job completion       while (TRUE) {         status <- dsHPC::dsHPC.status(job$job_id, return_result = TRUE)         if (status$status %in% c("COMPLETED", "CACHED")) {           return(status$result)         } else if (status$status == "FAILED") {           stop("Job failed: ", status$error_message)         }         Sys.sleep(2)       }     }   } else {     # Fallback to local computation     warning("dsHPC not available, computing locally")     # Local computation code   } }

what: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
title: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
text: server-side package providing DataSHIELD access High-Performance Computing capabilities.

title: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
text: dsHPC package enables DataSHIELD users submit computationally intensive functions HPC clusters, monitor job status, retrieve results efficiently. package provides interface job schedulers (currently Slurm) includes caching system avoid redundant computations. Key features include: Submit R functions executed HPC clusters Job status monitoring management Efficient caching results based function+arguments hashing Secure server-side operations within DataSHIELD framework Function wrapping utilities simplify HPC interactions

title: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
text: Initialize system dsHPC.init() Submit job dsHPC.submit() Check job status dsHPC.status() Retrieve results dsHPC.result()

title: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
text: Maintainer: David Sarrat González david.sarrat@isglobal.org Authors: Xavier Escribà-Montagut xavier.escriba@isglobal.org Juan R González juanr.gonzalez@isglobal.org

title: dsHPC: DataSHIELD Interface for High-Performance Computing — dsHPC-package
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Initialize the system dsHPC.init()  # Submit a job job <- dsHPC.submit(   func_name = "kmeans",   args = list(x = my_data, centers = 3),   package = "stats" )  # Check job status status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id)  # Get result (with waiting) result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id, wait_for_completion = TRUE) } # }

title: Submit a Function Execution as a Job by Name — dsHPC.submit_by_name
text: func_name Character string name function run. args List arguments pass function. object_hash Optional pre-computed hash function arguments. slurm_opts Optional list Slurm-specific options (partition, memory, time, cpus). use_cache Logical indicating whether reuse cached results. required_packages Character vector additional packages load. package Character string package name containing function. cache_only Logical indicating whether check cache without submitting job (default: FALSE).

title: Submit a Function Execution as a Job by Name — dsHPC.submit_by_name
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Initialize dsHPC first dsHPC.init()  # Submit a simple job job <- dsHPC.submit_by_name(   func_name = "mean",   args = list(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Submit a job with package function and custom Slurm options job <- dsHPC.submit_by_name(   func_name = "kmeans",   args = list(x = my_data_matrix, centers = 3),   package = "stats",   required_packages = c("stats"),   slurm_opts = list(     partition = "normal",     memory = "8G",     time = "02:00:00",     cpus = 2   ) ) } # } if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Initialize dsHPC first dsHPC.init()  # Submit a job job <- dsHPC.submit_by_name(   func_name = "mean",   args = list(x = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) )  # Check job status status <- dsHPC.status(job$job_id)  # Get result when job is complete if (status$status == "COMPLETED") {   result <- dsHPC.result(job$job_id)   print(result) } } # }
